No, that's not for caching access, but for lazy initialization of the `_method_cache` attribute.
The method cache should be specific to each `NodeVisitor`-derived type.

To do that, we could:
- Use a class attribute. 
  If we do that, then everyone implementing a `NodeVisitor`-derived class would have to define `_method_cache` class attribute, initialized to `{}`.
  As that's not very user-friendly, and would break existing implementations, I didn't do it.
- Use an instance attribute.
  The pythonic way would define the instance attribute with `self._method_cache = {}` in `NodeVisitor.__init__()`. If we do that, then everyone implementing a `NodeVisitor`-derived class has to call the parent constructor.
  For backward compatibility, I didn't do it.
- Use `__metaclass__`.
  Metaclasses are a nice way to customize the creation of a class, and would serve the purpose well.
  Unfortunately, they go a bit beyond simple python, and make the code a bit less maintainable.
  So I didn't use them either (I tested it, though, and can submit them).

What was implemented:
- I defined a default, `None`-initialized `_method_cache` at class level, which is only read.
  When executing `visit` for the first time, and reading `self._method_cache`, we actually read `self.__class__._method_cache`, which is `None`.
  When we set `self._method_name` to a default, empty `dict`, this effectively creates `self._method_name` at instance level.
  As `self._method_name` now exists, further reads will return the instance value, not the class value.
  I chose to not set `self.__class__._method_cache`, as this could have caused problems for users maintaining a class hierarchy of `NodeVisitor`.

For the current implementation:
Pros:
 - This ensures that the variable exists, even if the writer of a `NodeVisitor`-derived class forgot to define a `method_cache` class variable, or forgot to call the parent constructor.
 - This is backward-compatible with existing implementations (indeed, all tests still pass)
Cons:
 - This creates a `_method_cache` variable at instance level, where it would be better set at class level.
   This can be fixed by using `self.__class__` instead of `self`.
 - This is not very pythonic...

I did some additional performance checks (focused on the cache mechanism, and not using the generator):
```
10000 iterations: 15.567s on Original
10000 iterations: 11.181s on Method cache only, using class name as key (pull request contents)
10000 iterations: 10.957s on Method cache only, using class type as key
10000 iterations: 10.903s on Method cache only, define cache at __init__
10000 iterations: 10.785s on Method cache only, define cache in metaclass
```

So there's an important difference between `add_future` like this and `add_done_callback`. `add_future` schedules things such that another coroutine can run between our future finishing and an event hook firing. `add_done_callback` will execute the hook immediately after the future finishes.

Since event hooks tend to be concerned about things like timing, logging, etc., I think we actually *do* want to use `add_done_callback` with our event hooks. @abhinav would you agree with that?

I think I understand the general intent here, but part of me wonders if we wouldn't be better off in general leaning more towards a more liberal deprecation policy here (i.e. favoring development velocity over stability) by sticking to 2 minor releases as the minimum. I mainly think this because the primary cases where we bump up against the deprecation policy seem to be on the dev side (cant remove X yet) vs on the consumption side -
 largely because the warnings are pretty noisy and there's always some kind of alternative that can usually be easily adopted in lieu of the deprecated thing. this model might also encourage users to think about deprecations more often (ideally, every release) which seems like it'd be good in general too.

we can still apply our best judgement to the actual deprecations taking place tho to avoid egregious cases, but we'd just have more freedom to keep the codebase clean.

Generally, the agent user isn't interacting directly with the transaction anywhere, and instead uses these module-level functions (which can also be accessed from the "root" module, so e.g. `elasticapm.mark()`). There's two reasons for this API design:

 * it makes for a nicer API in my opinion. Compare

       elasticapm.mark('my-mark')

   with

       transaction = get_transaction()
       transaction.mark('my-mark')
 * `get_transaction()` is not a public API (as in not documented, there's not really anything we can do to stop people from using it), because at the moment it is a very thin wrapper around a thread local. Yes, a change to the Active Operational Dataset can only be done by receiving a new Active Operational Dataset via a MGMT_ACTIVE_SET request message.  As I mentioned before, the change needs to be all or nothing.  If you break up the operational dataset, then you have the same Active Dataset Timestamp in both the Active and Pending Operational Datasets, however the Active Dataset Timestamp represents different datasets.  When the Pending Delay Timer expires, the Pending Dataset cannot be applied to the Active Dataset because the Active Timestamp does not advance.  As a result, if you are delaying a change to the Active Operational Dataset, you need to delay the entire dataset (not just a portion of it).
